en.extract:
The north Moluccan cicadabird (Edolisoma grayi) is a passerine bird in the family Campephagidae that is found on Halmahera, the Bacan Islands, the Tukangbesi Islands and the island of Morotai in the northern Moluccas Islands of Indonesia. The species was formerly considered to be conspecific with the common cicadabird, now renamed the Sahul cicadabird.


Taxonomy
The north Moluccan cicadabird was formally described in 1861 by the English zoologist George Gray based on a specimen that had been collected on the island of Bacon which lies southwest of Halmahera in Indonesia. Gray coined the binomial name Campephaga melanotis. In 1879 the Italian zoologist Tommaso Salvadori coined the replacement name Edolisoma grayi as Gray's name was pre-occupied by Graucalus melanotis that had been introduced by John Gould in 1838. Gould's Graucalus melanotis is now considered to be a junior synonym of a subspecies of the black-faced cuckooshrike Coracina novaehollandiae melanops (Latham, 1801). The north Moluccan cicadabird was formerly treated as conspecific with the common cicadabird (now renamed the Sahul cicadabird) (Edolisoma tenuirostre).  It was elevated to species status based on differences in plumage and vocalization combined with the results of a molecular genetic study published in 2018. The genetic study found that the north Moluccan cicadabird was sister to the Sula cicadabird (Edolisoma suma).
Two subspecies are recognised.

E. g. pererratum Hartert, EJO, 1918 – Tukangbesi Islands (southeast of southeast Sulawesi)
E. g. grayi Salvadori, 1879 – Morotai to Bacan Islands (north Moluccas)


References

The '''north Moluccan cicadabird''' ('''''Edolisoma grayi''''') is a [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Campephagidae]] that is found on [[Halmahera]], the [[Bacan Islands]], the [[Tukangbesi Islands]] and the island of [[Morotai]] in the northern [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas Islands]] of Indonesia. The species was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the common cicadabird, now renamed the [[Sahul cicadabird]].

==Taxonomy==
The north Moluccan cicadabird was [[species description|formally described]] in 1861 by the English zoologist [[George Robert Gray|George Gray]] based on a specimen that had been collected on the island of [[Bacan Islands|Bacon]] which lies southwest of [[Halmahera]] in Indonesia. Gray coined the [[binomial name]] ''Campephaga melanotis''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gray | first=George Robert | author-link=George Robert Gray | date=1860 | publication-date=1861 | title=List of birds collected by Mr. Wallace at the Moluccia Islands, with the descriptions of new species, etc. | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | issue=Part 28 | pages=341–366 [353] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12866921 }} The title page is dated 1860 but the article was not published until the following year. See: {{Cite journal | last=Dickinson | first=Edward C. | date=2005 | title=The Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London, 1859–1900: an exploration of breaks between calendar years of publication | journal=Journal of Zoology | volume=266 | issue=4 | pages=427–430 | doi=10.1017/S0952836905007077}}</ref> In 1879 the Italian zoologist [[Tommaso Salvadori]] coined the replacement name ''Edolisoma grayi'' as Gray's name was pre-occupied by ''Graucalus melanotis'' that had been introduced by [[John Gould]] in 1838.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Salvadori | first=Tommaso | author-link=Tommaso Salvadori | year=1879 | title=Prodromus ornithologiae Papuasiae et Moluccarum | language=Latin | journal=Annali del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Genova | volume=15 | pages=32–48 [37] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/29859463 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=187 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480862 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1838 | title=A Synopsis of the Birds of Australia, and the Adjacent Islands | publisher=self | location=London | at=Part 4, Plate 54, Fig 3 and text | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47441702 }}</ref> Gould's ''Graucalus melanotis'' is now considered to be a [[junior synonym]] of a subspecies of the [[black-faced cuckooshrike]] ''Coracina novaehollandiae melanops'' ([[John Latham (ornithologist)|Latham]], 1801).<ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Greenway | editor2-first=James C. Jr | year=1960 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=9 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=172 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480847 }}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=28 September 2024 }}</ref> The north Moluccan cicadabird was formerly treated as [[conspecific]] with the common cicadabird (now renamed the [[Sahul cicadabird]]) (''Edolisoma tenuirostre'').  It was elevated to species status based on differences in plumage and vocalization combined with the results of a molecular genetic study published in 2018.<ref name=ioc/><ref name=pedersen>{{Cite journal | last1=Pedersen | first1=M.P. | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Joseph | first3=L. | last4=Rahbek | first4=C. | last5=Jønsson | first5=K.A. | date=2018 | title=Phylogeography of a 'great speciator' (Aves: ''Edolisoma tenuirostre'') reveals complex dispersal and diversification dynamics across the Indo-Pacific | journal=Journal of Biogeography | volume=45 | issue=4 | pages=826–837 | doi=10.1111/jbi.13182| bibcode=2018JBiog..45..826P | hdl=11250/2593769 | hdl-access=free }}</ref> The genetic study found that the north Moluccan cicadabird was [[sister taxon|sister]] to the [[Sula cicadabird]] (''Edolisoma suma'').<ref name=pedersen/>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>
* ''E. g. pererratum'' [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, EJO]], 1918 – [[Tukangbesi Islands]] (southeast of southeast [[Sulawesi]])
* ''E. g. grayi'' [[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]], 1879 – [[Morotai]] to [[Bacan Islands]] (north [[Maluku Islands|Moluccas]])
